meta.title: AGI Pathways: Tomorrow's Futures
meta.description: Build your path to a better AI future. A hands-on sandbox where you assemble strategies, test scenarios, and create real-world action plans.

landing.title: AGI Pathways: Tomorrow's Futures
landing.tagline: Build your path. Explore scenarios. Assemble what matters.
landing.description: Snap together your strategy in a hands-on sandbox. Build defences, test real threat pathways, and create a blueprint you can use in the real world — for policy, education, or your community.
landing.howToTryItStep1: Click “Enter the sandbox” to open the game.
landing.howToTryItStep2: Start in Zone 1: build your week and tap the People / Planet / Parity blocks to set priorities. Use the sliders to fine-tune.
landing.howToTryItStep3: Jump to Zone 3: choose what to protect (e.g. schools, democracy), pick a threat pathway, then write and save your Threat Blueprint.
landing.howToTryItStep4: Move between zones with Previous / Next or the zone number tabs. Your progress is saved automatically.
landing.howItWorksBody: Five build zones: assemble your future, wire up AI drivers, snap together threat pathways, build your defence stack, and craft your action plan. Your progress is saved in your browser — no login.
landing.disclaimer: This is an independent capstone project by Joshua Williams for the BlueDot Impact AGI Strategy Course; it is not associated with BlueDot Impact or its staff.
landing.aboutBody: Ages 8+. Build a Threat Card, a Defence Map, and a Personal Action Plan you can actually use — in class, at work, or in your community.

game.title: AGI Pathways
game.howToPlayIntro: Use the zones to build your strategy. Progress is saved in your browser.
game.howToPlayZone1: Zone 1: Build your week and tap People / Planet / Parity blocks; use sliders to fine-tune.
game.howToPlayZone3: Zone 3: Choose an asset and a pathway, then write and save your Threat Blueprint.
game.howToPlayNav: Use Previous / Next or the zone tabs above to move between zones.

1.title: Racing to a Better Future
1.intro: Visualise a future to aspire towards, explore how AI is shaping that future, and analyse the dilemmas we must overcome to steer towards a better future.

2.title: Drivers of AI Progress
2.intro: Wire up how AI gets better: compute, algorithms, and data. See how timelines change.

3.title: Pathways to Harm
3.intro: Choose what to protect. Snap together a threat scenario and build your Threat Card — a real blueprint you can use.

4.title: Defence in Depth
4.intro: Build your defence stack: prevent, constrain, withstand. Assemble your strategy like a Trailmakers build.

5.title: Start Contributing
5.intro: Craft your intervention and a concrete 3–6 month step plus 1–3 year direction. Take it into the real world.

unit1.storyDockPara1: You're the latest in about 8,000 generations of humans.
unit1.storyDockPara2: They built this world — its magic, beauty, and flaws.
unit1.storyDockPara3: Now we're living through one of the biggest technology changes ever.
unit1.storyDockPara4: What you and others decide about AI could shape the story for everyone who comes after.
unit1.lifeBoatTitle: Life Boat Builder – Your Future Week
unit1.lifeBoatPrompt: Imagine you +20 years from now, living a good, happy life. What does one week look like then?
unit1.worldFerryTitle: World Ferry Builder – Better Future for Society
unit1.worldFerryIntro: Over the next 20 years, about 2.7 billion people will join our world. Let's build a world we're proud to welcome them into.
unit1.agiRacePierTitle: AGI Race Pier – Who's Steering the Race?
unit1.agiRacePierPrompt: Different people and groups are racing to build powerful AI systems. Each one has different goals and pressures. For this game, choose one to ride along with and learn from.
unit1.actorReflectionPrompt: Why did you choose this character? What are they most worried about? What do they most want?
unit1.sectionImaginePara1: You're the product of 8,000 generations of humans. You were born into the civilization they built, with all its magic, beauty, and flaws. Their decisions shape every aspect of our world.
unit1.sectionImaginePara2: We're only the latest generation of humans, but we're living through one of the most significant technological transformations in the history of the universe. Our decisions today will have an immense impact on our own future and the future of all our descendants.
unit1.sectionImaginePara3: We may still be early in humanity's story. We have an amazing opportunity to steer this story towards wonder, greatness, and kindness. But that's not guaranteed.
unit1.sectionImaginePara4: In this unit, you'll visualise a future to aspire towards, you'll explore how AI is shaping that future, and you'll analyse the dilemmas and coordination failures we must overcome to steer towards a better future.
unit1.sectionImagineBullets: Throughout the rest of the course, you will: analyse the technical trends of AI progress and what this implies for future AI capabilities; develop a step-by-step story ("kill chain") for how those capabilities could cause harm to humanity; apply a "defence in depth" framework to analyse what needs to be built to defend against your kill chain and steer AI towards safe and beneficial outcomes; generate your own action plan for how you'll start contributing to making AI go well.
unit1.personalFutureHeading: What does a better future for yourself look like?
unit1.personalFutureIntro: In so many ways, human lives have been transformed in the past few centuries. In many ways, perhaps most, it's been for the better. However, there's a huge amount of work left to do to steer our future toward wiser, kinder, happier outcomes.
unit1.personalFuturePrompt: How old are you? Add 20 years to that number. Imagine a week in your life when you're that age, where you're living a good, happy life. Consider going for a short walk to reflect on this. Write below what that week looks like.
unit1.societalFutureHeading: What does a better future look like for society?
unit1.societalFutureIntro: Assuming current population trends, over the next 20 years, we will welcome 2.7 billion new people to our global community and lose 1.5 billion.
unit1.societalFuturePrompt: If you felt pride in welcoming these new humans into our world, what would the world look like? Consider factors such as politics, the economy, social order and community, technological progress, and the environment. Set your priorities (People, Planet, Parity) below and add notes.
unit1.sectionSteering: Steering the Race to AGI (Course Materials)
unit1.steeringPara1: You've just imagined futures worth protecting. But those futures aren't guaranteed — they depend in part on the decisions being made right now by the people racing to build AGI, who often have incentives that push away from thoughtful development.
unit1.steeringPara2: Here, you'll learn more about the actors shaping the future of AI, including their goals and constraints.
unit1.resourceRandSeekingStability: Seeking stability in the competition for AI advantage (RAND)
unit1.charactersInstruction: Choose one character from the list below and read their character card. Then write 2–3 paragraphs describing your character's main motivations, capabilities, and external pressures/constraints.
unit1.charactersNote: Note that these cards are illustrative — they do not capture the true complexity of these characters.
unit1.characterNotesPlaceholder: Write 2–3 paragraphs about your character's motivations, capabilities, and external pressures/constraints...
unit1.societalSliders: Set how much the world should care about each. Move the sliders or tap the blocks below.
unit1.tapToAdjust: Tap block to boost; slider to fine-tune.

unit2.triadIntro: AI keeps getting better because we give it: more computer power (compute), smarter training tricks (algorithms), and lots of examples (data). Together, these three are called the AI Triad.
unit2.hardwareGrowthLabel: How fast does computer power get cheaper?
unit2.algoGrowthLabel: How much better do our AI training tricks get each year?
unit2.tooCheapToMeterWarning: At this level, super-smart AI could become "too cheap to meter" — almost anyone could afford to use it for big projects, good or bad.
unit2.timesBetterPerDollar: Your AI gets {{count}}× better per dollar each year.
unit2.explosionQuestion: If AIs can help design better AIs, what happens?
unit2.explosionCaption: An intelligence explosion is when AI becomes so good at improving itself that it gets much smarter, very fast.
unit2.tonerQuestion1: How far can we get with current AI designs?
unit2.tonerQuestion2: How much can AI improve itself?
unit2.tonerQuestion3: Will future AI be more like tools or agents?
unit2.timelineBlurbShort: You think powerful AI might arrive fairly soon and be quite agentic. That could be exciting and also risky.
unit2.timelineBlurbMedium: You think transformative AI could arrive on a medium timeline, with a mix of tools and agents. Plenty of time to prepare — but not infinite.
unit2.timelineBlurbLong: You think we have a long runway before superhuman AI. That could mean more time to get things right — or more time for surprises.
unit2.timelineBlurbUncertain: You're uncertain about when and how AI will get much smarter. Staying curious and adaptable is key.
unit2.technicalIntro: What should we do if superhuman intelligence becomes "too cheap to meter"? In this unit, we examine why AI capabilities keep getting better: cheaper and more plentiful compute, and better algorithms. We then analyse what that implies for the rate of future AI capabilities, and how quickly those capabilities will spread to many actors all over the world.
unit2.defFLOP: FLOP: a single basic mathematical operation (like addition or multiplication) that a computer performs. The total number of FLOP is a measure of computational work done during AI training.
unit2.defComputeEfficiency: Compute efficiency is a measure of how "good" an AI model you get with a given financial investment. E.g. if OpenAI spends $500M on compute to train their next AI model, how "good" will it be?
unit2.defHardwarePricePerf: Hardware price-performance measures the amount of computational resources (FLOP) available for a given financial investment. E.g. how many FLOP would OpenAI get with a $500M investment into NVIDIA H200 AI chips running for 1 month? This is improving at ~1.4x/year (i.e. you get 1.4x more FLOP per $ each year) due to Moore's Law.
unit2.defAlgorithmicEfficiency: Algorithmic efficiency measures how "good" of an AI model you get with a given number of FLOP. E.g. if OpenAI run their NVIDIA H200 chips for 1 month training an AI model (~10^26 FLOP), how "good" will that AI model be? This is improving at ~3x/year (i.e. you need 3x less FLOP to achieve the same capability each year).
unit2.defFormula: Compute efficiency (Capability/$) = Hardware price-performance (FLOP/$) × Algorithmic efficiency (Capability/FLOP). This is improving at ~4x/year (i.e. you need 4x less $ to achieve the same capability each year).
unit2.resScalingStateOfPlay: Scaling: the state of play in AI (One Useful Thing)
unit2.goodQuote: Let an ultraintelligent machine be defined as a machine that can far surpass all the intellectual activities of any man however clever. Since the design of machines is one of these intellectual activities, an ultraintelligent machine could design even better machines; there would then unquestionably be an 'intelligence explosion.' — Irving J Good, 1965
unit2.resUncharted: The most important time in history: AGI/ASI (Uncharted Territories)
unit2.resLongTimelines: Long timelines to advanced AI (Helen Toner)
unit2.sectionAccelerate: Will AI progress accelerate even faster?
unit2.accelerateIntro: Helen Toner's piece asks 3 fundamental questions about the trajectory of AI development: 1. How far can we get with the current AI paradigm? 2. How much can AI improve itself? 3. Will future AIs be tools we use, or agents that can act without us? The answers will determine whether, and how soon, transformative AI will arrive.
unit2.resUnresolvedDebates: Unresolved debates about the future of AI (Helen Toner)
unit2.resAsterisk: Common ground between AI 2027 and long timelines (Asterisk)

unit3.horizonOverlookTitle: Horizon Overlook – Map What's at Stake
unit3.horizonIntro: The future could be amazing. But that future is not guaranteed.
unit3.horizonPrompt: Pick 3–5 things you care most about protecting. Drag them onto your shield.
unit3.shieldBoardHint: These are the parts of the future you most want to protect.
unit3.assetHumanAgencyDignityDesc: People's ability to decide their own lives
unit3.coalitionIntro: You're not just picking "an evil AI" or "a bad government" — you're building a misaligned coalition: humans and AIs acting together in harmful ways.
unit3.governanceInsight: Sometimes people use AI to do harmful things (misuse). Sometimes AI systems themselves act in harmful ways (misalignment). Often it's messy coalitions of humans and AIs together. For this game, we treat them all as "misaligned coalitions".
unit3.pathwayPrompt: Based on what you know now, which pathway worries you the most for the future you built in Unit 1?
unit3.gatePowerSummary: A small group uses very powerful AI to control information, watch everyone, and stay in charge forever.
unit3.gateDriftSummary: Most jobs and decisions are done by AI. People have less and less say in how the world works.
unit3.gateBioSummary: AI tools help someone design a very dangerous disease. It spreads faster and is harder to stop than anything we've seen before.
unit3.gateGridSummary: AI is used to attack the systems that keep lights on, water clean, and food moving, causing long, serious disruptions.
unit3.threatRunPrompt: Choose how your coalition attacks: pick one asset at risk, one attack path, and one objective.
unit3.powerAttack2: Use AI surveillance to crush opposition
unit3.driftAttack1: Replace most jobs with AI systems quickly
unit3.driftAttack2: Shift key decisions from humans to AI advisors
unit3.bioAttack1: Use AI tools to design dangerous pathogens
unit3.bioAttack2: Help more people learn harmful bio techniques
unit3.gridAttack1: Hack power and water control systems
unit3.objMaximizeProfit: Maximize profit no matter the cost
unit3.objPushIdeology: Push an ideology at all costs
unit3.threatCardTemplate: The [ACTOR] with [CAPABILITY] and [MOTIVATION] attacks [ASSET] by [ATTACK PATHWAY] in order to [OBJECTIVE].
unit3.chooseAsset: What are you protecting? Pick your asset.
unit3.threatCardDescription: What could happen? (Use this in real discussions or policy work.)
unit3.assetBlocks: Choose one or more assets to protect
unit3.realWorldNote: This becomes a real-world artifact: use it in essays, presentations, or community discussions.
unit3.mapThreatPara1: The future could be amazing. You described it yourself in the first unit. That future might not happen by default. We have a tremendous opportunity and responsibility to make that future a reality for ourselves, everyone we know and love, and the rest of humanity.
unit3.mapThreatPara2: If we don't know what threatens that future, we're flying blind. Here, we'll offer a sketch of the territory: the things we're protecting, who or what might attack them, and how they might try. This is not intended to be a perfect overview — treat it like a starting point for your thinking.
unit3.reframingNote: Reframing AGI threat models: some researchers argue that the misuse vs misalignment distinction is less useful than focusing on "misaligned coalitions" — groups of humans and AIs that attempt to grab power in illegitimate ways. Technical and governance responses can be similar either way.
unit3.resColdTakes: AI could defeat all of us combined (Cold Takes)
unit3.prioritisingIntro: In this section, you will choose ONE threat pathway to investigate further. Here are actors we might be concerned about (capability and/or motivation to use AI to cause harm): 1. Misaligned AI — systems that act against the interests of humanity. 2. Powerful human actors (e.g. corporate CEOs, military and political leaders). 3. Malevolent nation states. 4. Terrorist groups and doomsday cults.
unit3.howCauseHarm: How might they cause catastrophic harm? They could use information warfare and conventional military force to overthrow democratic governments; do cyberattacks on critical infrastructure (water, energy, food); design and release viruses worse than SARS-CoV-2; or default incentives could lead to bad outcomes even without malicious intent.
unit3.exerciseZoom: Exercise: Zooming into one threat. Based on your current understanding, which threat pathway is of most concern? Consider likelihood and impact. We don't expect detailed thinking yet — consider this the beginning of your thinking.
unit3.threatScenarioTemplate: Write your own threat scenario using this template: The [ACTOR] with [CAPABILITY] and [MOTIVATION] attacks [ASSET] by [ATTACK PATHWAY] in order to [OBJECTIVE].
unit3.option1Body: A small group gains control of frontier AI systems and uses them to manipulate elections through micro-targeted disinformation, fake evidence, and synthetic media so convincing that truth becomes unknowable. They deploy AI-powered surveillance that tracks every digital interaction, predicts dissent before it emerges, and automatically suppresses opposition. Within months, autonomous weapons patrol the streets, facial recognition determines access to resources, and AI advisors whisper into the ears of every official — all controlled by those who seized the models first. Democratic institutions become theatrical performances while real power flows through algorithmic systems accountable to no one. Whether it's a government using AI to become permanently unremovable, a corporation achieving regulatory capture through superintelligent lobbying, or an AI system itself accumulating power while maintaining a facade of servitude, the result is the same: the permanent concentration of control in hands that will never voluntarily let go.
unit3.option2Body: As AI systems become cheaper and more capable than humans at nearly every task, companies face overwhelming pressure to automate. First it's customer service and data entry, then management and creative work, eventually even governance and resource allocation. This could lead to humans losing economic leverage as their labour becomes worthless; our ability to influence culture might degrade as AI generates all the content we consume; our political power could dissipate as governments pivot to AI for tax revenue, administration, and security. We might wake up one day to find ourselves comfortable but irrelevant — living in a world we no longer understand or control, where the economy optimises for AI activities rather than human needs.
unit3.option3Body: Advanced AI could dramatically lower the barriers to creating pandemic pathogens. Today, designing a novel virus requires rare expertise, expensive equipment, and years of work. In a few years, AI could walk someone through synthesising a pathogen more transmissible than measles and deadlier than rabies. Unlike natural pandemics that evolve toward transmission over lethality, an engineered pathogen could be optimised for maximum harm — combining long asymptomatic periods with high mortality, resistance to existing treatments, or even targeting specific genetic markers. The same AI capabilities that accelerate beneficial biomedical research could be misused by state bioweapons programmes, terrorist groups, or disturbed individuals to create humanity's final pandemic.
unit3.option4Body: Modern infrastructure runs on outdated industrial control systems and unpatched software that were never designed for internet connectivity. AI could systematically map these vulnerabilities across utilities, hospitals, and supply chains, then coordinate attacks that would take human hackers months to plan and execute. We've already seen previews: Colonial Pipeline, NotPetya, ransomware forcing hospitals to divert ambulances. AI-enabled attackers could compromise the industrial control systems that run power plants, the SCADA networks that manage water treatment, and the logistics software that routes food and medicine — enough to cause sustained disruptions that compound over weeks. When the power grid has rolling blackouts, water pressure drops, and just-in-time supply chains fail, even a wealthy nation starts to fray at the edges.

unit4.fleetGovGuardDesc: Few government-backed projects hold almost all the powerful AI. They only build more when they believe it's safe, and they lock everything down very tightly.
unit4.fleetCrownIntellectDesc: We race to build one super-smart AI that really follows our goals, then let it help lead the way and keep dangerous AIs from appearing.
unit4.fleetShieldNetDesc: Lots of people and places have AI, but we pour huge effort into shields, early warning, and safety so bad uses don't work.
unit4.strategyNotesPrompt: Why did you pick this mix? What worries you about the others?
unit4.killChainTitle: Kill Chain Dock – Your Threat's Journey
unit4.killChainIntro: Here's how your misaligned coalition might try to carry out their plan, step by step. Now we'll see where defences could stop them.
unit4.defenceRingIntro: Place defence cards on each ring. Prevent = stop dangerous AI from being trained. Constrain = keep trained AIs under control. Withstand = make society resilient if all else fails.
unit4.pppFeedbackLabel: Your defences and who they protect
unit4.focalLayerPrompt: No one can fix everything at once. For your threat scenario, which layer would you most want to improve?
unit4.focalConstrain: Constrain what the trained AI can do
unit4.focalUpgradePrompt: Upgrade 1–2 defence cards for this layer, or add a note on how you'd strengthen it.
unit4.focalReflectionPrompt: What is this layer trying to stop or protect? Why did you choose this layer?
unit4.successIntro: One way to break down existing AGI strategies is into three broad buckets. This is a gross over-simplification, but it captures the essence of the main "camps" for how to make AI go well.
unit4.strategy1Body: This strategy argues for centralising control over compute and frontier AI model weights into a small number of Government-backed "AGI Projects". These projects would coordinate or keep each other in check via coercion, surveillance and deterrence. Prominent proposals: "The Project" (Aschenbrenner), "CERN for AI", "Chips for Peace" (O'Keefe), "Technical Requirements for Halting Dangerous AI Activities" (Barnett et al.), "Superintelligence Strategy" (Hendrycks et al.). Proponents believe current race dynamics will push towards AI systems that could cause human extinction; they don't believe aligned superintelligence or defences against misaligned superintelligence are feasible in the short term. Concerns: extreme concentration of power, intractability of government control, risk that other actors build dangerous AI anyway.
unit4.strategy2Title: 2: Hand over control to aligned superintelligence
unit4.strategy2Body: This strategy argues that building superintelligence is inevitable and that only a superintelligent AI could steer us to a utopian future and protect humanity. Proponents believe "AI alignment" is possible. Some argue the aligned superintelligence should gain a "decisive strategic advantage" to prevent anyone else from building a misaligned superintelligence. This is often an implicit rather than explicit strategy. "There's going to be a handoff, where humanity hands off control to transformative AI at some point." — Anthropic co-founder Tom Brown.
unit4.strategy3Body: This strategy argues for widespread access to AGI and compute, with tremendous investment in defensive technologies. Proponents want no single actor to have too much power. Prominent proposals: "d/acc" (Vitalik Buterin), "def/acc" (Matt Clifford), "Differential Technological Development" (Nick Bostrom). In the future envisaged, protective technologies outpace destructive ones (e.g. rapid pathogen detection, better cybersecurity). Critiques: we may not be able to build or sustain defences; the defender must fix all vulnerabilities while an attacker needs one; defence may require far more resources than attack.
unit4.strategyNote: Some people believe we should pursue all three: slow down via government projects in the short term, build defences as capabilities diffuse in the medium term, and in the long term relinquish control to an aligned superintelligence.
unit4.layer1Body: The first layer is prevention: not training dangerous AI systems in the first place. Interventions: export controls on advanced AI chips, international agreements not to train past certain red lines, "safe by design" AI, norms against reckless racing. Prevention feels cleanest but is hard — there is intense commercial and national competition. Resources: AI Treaty (aitreaty.org), The Project (situational-awareness.ai).
unit4.layer2Body: When AI companies train their next system, they don't know what it will be capable of or how dangerous it will be; they discover that during and after training, then implement safeguards. So if a company trains a dangerous system, how will they know, and what might they do about it? Resources: What is AI alignment? (BlueDot), AI Control (BlueDot), RAND research brief.
unit4.layer3Body: If a dangerous model is trained and bypasses or escapes safeguards, we need to harden society: pandemic-proof detection and containment, critical infrastructure secure against physical and cyber attack, democratic systems and information ecosystems that help people make sense of reality. Resources: d/acc 2 (Vitalik).
unit4.buildingIntro: In the last unit you created a threat scenario and a kill chain. Read about each layer of defence in the next 3 sections, then apply ONE layer to slow down or stop the attack. Prevent: stop training/creation of the AI system with capabilities required for your attack. Constrain: if a dangerous AI is trained, how could the company constrain its ability to cause harm? Withstand: if dangerous AI escapes all controls, how could we make society more resilient?

unit5.contributionRadarPrompt: Which kind of work do you want to focus on first? Pick one sector where you could most help defend against the threat you built in Unit 3.
unit5.sectorPolicyDesc: Shape the rules, institutions, and incentives that guide how AI is built and used.
unit5.sectorTechnicalDesc: Work on the code and systems that make AI safer, more controllable, and better understood.
unit5.sectorFieldBuildingDesc: Grow the community of people who understand AI risks and work to make AI go well.
unit5.sectorCommunityDesc: Build bridges between labs, policymakers, funders, and communities so AI serves everyone fairly.
unit5.focusReasoningPrompt: Why did you pick this sector? (optional)
unit5.interventionDockPrompt: Pick one intervention to focus on first. This doesn't lock you in forever—it's just your current best guess.
unit5.researchSuccessPicture: What does success look like? If this intervention works really well in 5–10 years, what's different in the world?
unit5.researchHelpsAi: How does it help make AI go well? Which threat pathway does it help with? Which defence layer does it strengthen?
unit5.researchCurrentStatus: What's the current status? Is anyone already doing this? Governments, AI companies, nonprofits?
unit5.researchOrgsToWatch: Who's working on it? List 1–3 orgs or project types you think are relevant.
unit5.roleHangarTitle: Role Hangar – Choose & Customize Your Role
unit5.rolePolicyPilotWork: Policy and governance roles: advisor, researcher, advocate.
unit5.roleSafetyEngineerWork: Technical safety, ML engineer with alignment focus.
unit5.roleTechSocietyWork: Connects AI labs, policymakers, funders, and communities. Makes sure AI projects hear from and benefit real people, especially those most impacted.
unit5.roleCommunityNavigatorWork: Local organizer, librarian, teacher, community technologist.
unit5.shortTermPrompt: Pick 3–5 concrete steps you could realistically try in the next 3–6 months.
unit5.longTermPrompt: Imagine 1–3 years from now. What skills, projects, and relationships would you like to have built?
unit5.longTermSkillsPlaceholder: e.g. AI governance frameworks, partnership building, grant writing
unit5.longTermProjectsPlaceholder: e.g. Co-designed AI governance tools with communities and labs
unit5.longTermRelationshipsPlaceholder: e.g. Labs doing frontier safety, digital equity orgs, AI policy think tanks
unit5.alignmentNarrativePrompt: Write a short story (3–6 sentences) about how you, your chosen role, and your intervention help protect the future you designed in Unit 1 from the threat you explored in Unit 3.
unit5.launchMessage: You're now part of the small group of people working to make AI go well. This is just Level 1. Your next moves are in the real world.
unit5.missionBriefProgress: Your View on AI Progress & Timelines (Unit 2)
unit5.intvCityGuidelines: City-level AI Use Guidelines for Public Services
unit5.intvExplainableGuides: Public-Facing Explainable Guides on AI Risks
unit5.intvResponsibleToolkits: Responsible AI Toolkits for Local Orgs
unit5.chooseFocusIntro: In the last unit we explored what defences need to be built. Very few people in the world are trying to steer AGI towards beneficial outcomes — we estimate less than 2,000 full-time people. You could be one of them, but it requires stepping up, taking responsibility, and trying to solve new problems that don't have an instruction manual. In this unit you'll create an action plan. This is only the beginning; you're not expected to have a perfect answer yet.
unit5.resBecomePerson: Become a person who actually does things (Neel Nanda)
unit5.exercisePrioritise: Exercise: Prioritise a single intervention. Based on everything you've learnt, prioritise an intervention from the long list. Consider which would be effective against the kill chain you developed in Unit 3.
unit5.exerciseResearch: Do your own research: What does success look like with this intervention? How does it help make AI go well? What's the current status — are governments, AI companies or others already doing it? If not, why not? Which organisations are working on this that you could contribute to or join?
unit5.makePlanIntro: Synthesise everything you've learnt and develop your own action plan for how you could contribute to making AI go well. We don't expect a perfect plan — treat this as a first draft, good enough to get started.
unit5.aspirationNote: I aspire to become a Head of Tech & Society Partnerships, working at the intersection of labs, policymakers, funders, developers, and communities to ensure AI safely serves all people. I am particularly interested in how frontier AI reshapes power, labour, opportunity, and education; in creating governance tools that keep those impacts accountable and fair; and in partnerships and programs that make AI accessible to all, while teaching the harms and benefits of AI.
